data.title: U.S. GDP Beats And Monday Gaps Fill: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Index Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

This morning brought a fresh wave of optimism to Wall Street. First, traders received an update from the Trump Administration regarding Greenland. The recent rhetoric had posed significant sentiment hurdles, triggering repricings after a positive start to 2026.

The situation shifted yesterday afternoon when Trump posted that a framework for Greenland's strategic use by the US had been reached. While details remain unclear, the tone is calmer, easing fears of a NATO collapse or armed invasion. However, Greenland will likely continue to assert its sovereignty, meaning tensions could flare if the deal does not align with local views.

Combined with the latest data, US sentiment is back at highs. Q3 GDP beat expectations (4.4% vs 4.3% expected), arriving alongside slight misses in PCE data. This combination supports a soft-landing scenario, the most favorable outcome for stocks.

The ongoing earnings season is also leaving a decent impression. The key Magnificent 7 reports still face the test of extreme expectations, but the path should become clearer ahead of the January 28 FOMC meeting.

Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Tesla (TSLA), and ASML (ASML) report after the close on FOMC day, while Apple (AAPL) and Visa (V) follow on Thursday.

The picture is once again very green all around the market, with Meta leading to the upside (+4.22%) and Mag 7s sustaining well after their more volatile 2025 year-end. This expresses some repositioning ahead of their earnings next week. Energy minerals and producer manufacturing are among the lagging sectors.

Dive into our daily session charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500. Watch for pre-weekend volatility as the situation is not fully clear yet, and Iran remains a factor.

Dow Jones 4H Chart

Now easily brushing back above the 49,000 level, the DJIA is testing its all-time highs. The Morning 4H Candle just closed and expresses some form of momentum slowdown at previous highs, confirming more rangebound price action that could be expected all the way to next week's FOMC.

  • Breaking and closing above the 49,715 all-time highs could see follow through as long as a break occurs on strong volume. Momentum has gotten more bullish, but the TACO spike could slow down.
  • A pullback here could easily test the 49,150 level, which crosses with the 4H 50-period MA
  • Any session close below the 49,000 key level brings back seller strength, which could move towards the 4H 200-MA (48,620)

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Session Highs: 49,628
  • 49,650 to 49,670 Current ATH Resistance
  • All-time highs: 49,710
  • 50,000 Potential Psychological Resistance

Support Levels

  • 49,150 4H 50-period MA
  • Christmas ATH High Timeframe Momentum Pivot – 49,000
  • Intraday support 48,600 to 48,700
  • November ATH 48,300 to 48,500 Mini-Support
  • Psychological Support at 48,000
  • 45,000 psychological level (Main Support on higher timeframe)

Nasdaq 4H Chart

Nasdaq is once again stuck in its higher timeframe range (24,500 to 25,800) which has been containing its progress since its October drop. The two-session rebound is still very impressive, with the index up 2.50% during that span.

Looking further to higher timeframes, consolidations at relative all-time highs are a positive sign as it allows overbought conditions from the previous year to largely ease – conditions needed for clean breakouts.

Still, bulls will have to manage a daily close above the 25,700 resistance in order to generate further momentum and potentially push for a new all-time high record.

Next week's earnings will have a huge influence on stock movement. Keep a close attention to the Fed's communications next week.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Session Highs: 25,625
  • Intermediate Resistance 25,700 to 25,850
  • All-time high resistance zone 26,100 to 26,300
  • Current ATH: 26,182

Support Levels

  • Momentum Pivot 25,200 to 25,500 +/- 75 pts
  • Session lows: 25,425
  • Minor Support: 25,000 to 25,250
  • 24,500 Main support
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 4H Chart

The S&P 500 is about 100 points higher compared to our past-day analysis, but the index is expressing some technical doubts as it faces a retest of its Monday futures gap (6,930).

The current dragonfly doji candle indicates decent bull resilience but generally balanced action, which could easily lead to a retest of the 6,880 to 6,900 pivot area.

For bull/bear indications, traders can spot whether the action remains above the 4H 50-period MA (6,915) – closing below on high volume can prompt a further correction to 6,830-6,850, past day support levels.

Don't forget to stay close to the headlines, as the World Economic Forum is still ongoing!

Safe Trades!

data.title: Core PCE Inflation At 2.8% In November, As Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, the core PCE price index, rose 2.8% year-over-year in November. This was consistent with the forecast and a slight pickup from October's 2.7% reading. On a monthly basis, the core index was up 0.2%, as expected.

The headline index was up 2.8% year-over-year, up from 2.7% in October and in line with the forecast. On a monthly basis, the index was up 0.2%, as expected.

PCE Price Index

Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) measures and tracks changes for all domestic personal consumption. Core PCE measures the changes in personal consumption less food and energy, making it less volatile than the headline PCE. The PCE Price Index is calculated using PCE data and is a key way to measure changes in purchasing trends and inflation.

The adjacent thumbnail gives us a close-up of the trend in YoY core PCE since January 2012. The first string of red data points highlights the 12 consecutive months when core PCE hovered in a narrow range around its interim low.

The second string highlights the lower range from late 2014 through 2015. Core PCE shifted higher in 2016 with a decline in 2017, 2019, and 2020, with a major jump in 2022.

The following chart shows the monthly year-over-year change in the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) price index since 2000. An overlay of the core PCE price index is also included, with the highlighted line on the chart representing the 2% benchmark for core inflation.

For a long-term perspective, here are the same two metrics spanning five decades.

Inflation: Fed's 2% Target

The Fed traditionally uses the PCE Price Index as their preferred inflation gauge; however, another common measure of inflation is the Consumer Price Index.

In their latest meeting, the Fed implemented a widely anticipated 25 basis point cut to the federal funds rate (FFR), bringing it to a range of 3.50%-3.75%. This is the third consecutive rate cut and puts the central bank’s range at its lowest level since November 2022.

The statement from the meeting revealed the committee believes "inflation has moved up since earlier in the year and remains somewhat elevated," but that they are strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2% objective.

The Fed will meet again next week, where they are expected to hold rates steady. At the time of writing, the CME FedWatch Tool shows a 95% likelihood the Fed will hold rates where they are versus a 5% likelihood of a 25 basis point reduction.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Back To Fundamentals: 3 Resilient Picks For 2026
data.description:
Last year, I wrote an article titled "Back to fundamentals: 3 Picks for 2025". The picks were Nvidia (NVDA), PDD Holdings (PDD), and Pinterest (PINS). You can tell simply by those picks that my perspective going into the year surrounded Growth At a Reasonable Price (GARP), without much respect to risk or sleep-well-at-night (SAWN) status. Feel free to read the article and its reasoning. While the picks have fared decently (as an average), I'll personally be waiting 3-5 years to judge the performance of these companies and my call.

I'd like to select a new set of companies for 2026. I'll admit I have become less adventurous, having actually sold both my PDD and Pinterest holdings at reasonable gain through the year, despite truly enjoying being a shareholder and still believing in their business model and potential. Frankly, both companies continue to deserve higher valuations. For example, PDD's stock is $106, with $58B in net cash. This leads to a forward EV/EBIT of 6.8x and nearly $6B annually simply from interest payments. I could go on. As for Pinterest, the company grew revenues 16.8% in the last quarter and is trading at 13.1x forward earnings, with a large net cash position and a user base growing at 12% annually in monthly actives. These numbers continue to not make much sense, which is why we should be giving the market time to figure out the value here or for the companies to produce the earnings I expect they will.

Regardless, here are my top picks for 2026, with a strong emphasis on moats.

Meta Platforms (META)

Meta Platforms was a laggard in 2025 and has fallen through 2026.

I've written about Meta before, highlighting its several social media platforms, its monetization of Threads and WhatsApp, and its budding smart glasses business, through which the company expects to deliver Personal Superintelligence For All. You can review that in-depth article here.

  • Meta Platforms: Future Bright in Several Spots, Valuation More Than Acceptable for Long-Term Shareholders

Today, I want to focus on two key reasons Meta Platforms remains a Strong Buy for me.

1. Accelerating Business

Given what I've covered in previous articles, this business has extremely strong fundamentals. The company's main business metric, its Family of Apps Monthly Active Users (MAU), grew 7.6% year over year in the last quarter, accelerating from 6.4% annually from the quarter before.

This culminated in revenue growth of 26.25%. If nothing else, you can hang onto that as a huge lever in favor of Meta shareholders. No other companies in the Magnificent 7 are growing like that, except Nvidia (NVDA). The law of large numbers clearly applies at much larger numbers than Meta's current revenue figures.

The company did take a huge one-time tax charge anticipating the effects of the One Big Beautiful Bill, but the market didn't seem to react to that upon the earnings release. Instead, the market has been fixated on Meta's lagging AI competitiveness. Meta has been very slow getting its AI MAUs and their traffic volume up to levels seen at Google.

Regardless, Meta's business is social media, not AI - and in that regard, 26% revenue growth is astounding.

2. Valuation

This bull market has been expensive since it was born in late October 2022. As such, one would expect Meta to be valued at least in line with the market and/or its megacap peers.

Instead, the company is trading at 19.9x 2026 earnings, or if you prefer operating cash flow, Meta is trading at 12x 2026 OCF. Note below that Meta's long-term operating cash flow multiple is over 17x. A reversion to trend here by 2027 would get us to a price target of $1067, counting dividends. This does not count any AI progress the company is actively attempting.

Risks

An ordinary recession is always a risk for Meta's shareholders in the short term. Companies of all sizes cut their ad budgets, directly impacting Meta Platforms. Yet, had you bought Meta at its September 2021 peak before the 2022 bear market, you'd have returned 12% annually for 4 years, slightly outperforming the S&P 500.

One could also argue Meta is at risk of disruption from AI. Theoretically, social media users could get AI to auto-generate content. Obviously I don't buy this for a minute - social media is social in nature - comments sections, drama, niche interests, and your personal friends group are why I believe users continue to grow at Meta's platforms. I'm going to go as far as coining the term "cultural media" which more appropriately describes what happens on "social" media, while also indicating Meta's significance and embeddedness in the world.

Mastercard (MA)

Meta Platforms is my growth play idea for 2026 because the market has truly discounted its growth without AI, let alone what could be possible with AI (such as embedded in their smart glasses).

As a second idea for "secured growth", in other words, growth that is risk-adjusted, investors can hardly do better than Mastercard at current levels. Mastercard is the world's second-largest credit card company, beaten only by Visa (V). The company supports its merchants with a very strong and budding software side business called Value-Added Services and Solutions. Within this business line, which Mastercard has been cross-selling to its merchants, growth has been a steady 22-25% annually.

Importantly, Mastercard carries no debt associated with its cardholders - the banks carry such debt. Mastercard simply connects the cardholder's bank to the merchant's bank through quite a complex and irreplicable payments network.

Valuation

While Mastercard has never really been dirt cheap, its valuation recently has become enticing, especially in the face of all-time stock market highs and several international pressures and drama we're all already aware of.

Apart from the bear market of 2022, Mastercard has not been this cheap since 2017. Better still, the company comes with little debt given its worldwide embeddedness, standing at 3.5x long-term debt/EBITDA, and the company is one of the most widely held companies among super-investors.

A reversion to trend by the end of 2027 would get us to a share price of $875, counting dividends and assuming 16% OCF growth.

Risks

The company's risks mainly include greater regulatory oversight or a consumer-led recession. Both such risks are par for the course for almost any publicly traded company.

Waste Connections (WCN)

My risk aversion from 2025 has amplified into 2026. To be frank, I don't care how well amazing opportunities in little-known companies do - I know I'll be uncomfortable holding them if I don't feel they are monumental and important companies (one of my Five Factors). Further, I've decided to assume risks I've been worried about will indeed happen and adjust my portfolio as such, instead of hoping they don't. Therefore, I expect a Chinese blockade of Taiwan in the coming five years, something I covered in detail in my October article on Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), below.

  • Three Serious Problems Owning Taiwan Semiconductor

Further, as mentioned previously, indices are at or near all-time highs, with the (SP500) quite far above its long-term averages.

As such, I believe it's totally reasonable and prudent to own something uncorrelated with all the following three issues: a problem in Taiwan, a consumer or economic recession, and a market multiple contraction. The company that perhaps most diversifies away from these risks while maintaining more than reasonable growth is Waste Connections.

In short, Waste Connections has long-term contracts with municipalities to pick up their garbage and manage it at Waste Connections' landfills. The company has expanded into methane collection from their landfills. It competes mostly in a duopoly with Waste Management (WM), but in reality Waste Management focuses on urban centers, while Waste Connections focuses on rural areas. Both companies are rather insulated from each other, in that establishing a second landfill in a given area will always face the epitome of NIMBYism, while establishing the first one is welcomed by the municipality.

Further, Waste Connections maintains a slightly different business model. In rural areas, the cost per pickup is larger, given the greater distances the garbage trucks must travel, while in urban areas the cost per pickup is less due to urban density. As such, Waste Management encroaching on Waste Connection's rural areas would be a double negative effect of lowering margins while attempting to establish alternative landfills to Waste Connections, something no one wants. As such, Waste Connections' growth has gone relatively unhindered.

Risks

Waste Connections will likely always be here across North America to pick up our garbage, and the chance of disruption from AI or municipal contract failure due to recessions is about zero. I specifically bought this company's shares recently because I could hardly conceive of any risks. I believe Waste Connections largest risk is either mismanagement, something any company faces, or valuation concerns.

Valuation

Waste Connections has been historically valued around 36x adjusted earnings. At the outset, this will be too rich for many value-based investors. However, when you consider the indestructibility of the company in the long term, a valuation like this, which mirrors valuations for Mastercard, begins to make sense. Further, it's somewhat helpful to know that the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation Trust - a charity whose investments are designed to survive and grow long-term—maintains a 1% position in WCN and a whopping 17% position in WM, a strong nod to the industry's long-term survivability.

A reversion to historical valuations, which can likely be expected given the lack of AI threats, would push the stock price to $354, including dividends, a fantastic return on a risk-adjusted basis.

Conclusion

As I've pivoted from growth to risk-adjusted growth in the last 7 or 8 months, my perspectives on my portfolio have evolved in unexpected ways. This has led me from a total return-at-all-costs mentality to a risk-adjusted total return mentality.

Consequently, I've been prioritizing these three companies, along with two more I hope to write about in the coming weeks, in order to capture adequate growth for my portfolio while mitigating risks I see in the current environment, which I really dislike.

The combination of Meta Platforms, Mastercard, and Waste Connections casts a wide net across tollbooth-like companies with entrenched businesses whose industries face massive barriers to entry, where AI is of no use in breaking down those barriers. I'm rating all these companies a Strong Buy, indicating I have purchased or added to all three in the last few days. These three companies alone sum to over 30% of my portfolio, and I may intend to purchase more should valuations allow it.

data.title: European Wax Center: Despite The Pain, This Prospect Offers Upside
data.description:
Even though I have established for myself a good track record over the long run, I'm not going to sit here and say that my timing is always right. A good example of my timing not playing out the way that I thought it would involves European Wax Center, Inc. (EWCZ), a rather interesting business that focuses on operating locations dedicated to providing waxing services. Back in July of last year, I decided to upgrade the stock from a "Hold" to a "Buy." This was driven by improved clarity when it came to future growth and robust cash flows that the company was exhibiting. Since that time, however, the market has disagreed with me. Shares have been pushed lower to the tune of 19.8%. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is up 8.9%.

As painful as this is to see, I believe the fundamentals are working in my favor. Yes, revenue is still dropping, but bottom-line results have improved quite a bit. We are also seeing some improvements in same store sales, which is excellent. And when it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management projected that profits would be higher than they were the previous year. Add on top of this just how cheap shares are, and I do think that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate is the right move.

This Hurt

Just like waxing sometimes does, or at least from what I've been told since I have never had it done personally, European Wax Center has experienced some pain as of late. Take the most recent quarter as an example. This involves the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. According to management, revenue amounted to $54.2 million. This was actually down from the $55.4 million the company reported a year earlier. Unfortunately, system-wide sales contracted slightly from $240.2 million to $238.2 million. This came as the number of locations in operation pulled back from 1,064 to 1,053.

This drop was intentional. Management has acknowledged that the company is currently in the early stages of a transformation aimed at improving operations. While the company still believes in long-term growth prospects, it blames a weak consumer environment for its troubles. Many franchisees ended up pausing their new center growth plans as the company sought to stabilize operations. And because of declining transactions and profitability, the company has watched as the number of locations has dropped. In fact, for 2025 in its entirety, the company told investors that they would close between 35 and 40 locations. That compares to just 12 openings. The good news, however, is that the closing count actually marks a big improvement from the 40 to 60 locations that the company previously anticipated closing for the year.

Normally when revenue drops, profitability also takes a hit. But that's not what we saw here. In fact, net income jumped from $1.5 million to $3.8 million. Some of this was because of a decline in advertising revenue. But the bigger impact involved a decline in selling, general, and administrative costs. Management chalked this up to lower payroll and benefits costs, a decrease in professional fees, a reduction in corporate marketing, and lower provisions for bad debts. There was also a decline in recruiting costs. All of these are good signs in the sense that they show that management is making progress on transforming the business so that it can thrive in the long run. The fact that same-center sales actually increased by 0.2% year over year also helped, since it means more revenue dollars coming in relative to fixed costs. That is definitely friendly from a margin standpoint.

Other profitability metrics have also improved. Adjusted net income, for instance, grew from $9.4 million to $10.7 million. Operating cash flow popped from $14.9 million to $17.3 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $11.6 million to $14.2 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company increased from $18.4 million to $20.2 million. All of this is great to see, and what's even better is that this was not a blip on the radar. In the chart below, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue declined once again. But profits and every single cash flow metric for the company improved.

Management said that system-wide sales for the 2025 fiscal year as a whole would likely be somewhere between $945 million and $948 million. To put that in perspective, for 2024, they amounted to $951.3 million. That's not a horrible decline, but it's a drop all the same. And as a result, management expects that revenue will have dropped from $216.9 million to between $206 million and $208 million. This is even though same-center revenue is expected to inch up by between 0.1% and 0.3%, with the drop actually being attributable to the aforementioned decline in location count.

Management's own guidance called for EBITDA to range between $72 million and $74 million for the year. They also said that adjusted net profits would be somewhere between $33 million and $35 million. And if we project out results based on what we have seen already for 2025, we would likely get adjusted operating cash flow of somewhere in the range of $59.3 million. For a company, this size, with a market capitalization of $214.7 million and an enterprise value of $539.8 million, this is a lot in the way of profitability.

When it comes to valuing the company, comparative analysis is not really feasible. There aren't any good comparable firms to stack up against this. The closest you could get would be beauty-related companies, but even those have some major differences. Because of this, I think that just analyzing the valuation of the company in a vacuum is more appropriate. Doing this, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Honestly, these are really low multiples. And even though financial performance has been mixed as of late, the firm still has a net leverage ratio of about 4.09. That's not great, but it's not so high that I am concerned. That's especially in light of the improvements the company has seen on the bottom line, with management working to transform the business.

When it comes to 2026, we don't have any detailed guidance. However, the company has acknowledged that it is facing continuing challenges when it comes to new guest acquisition and when it comes to certain regional markets that it operates in. This means that management still has progress that needs to be made. Having said that, the company does expect to achieve a return to unit growth by the end of this year, which, when combined with management's continued push to improve profitability by cutting costs, does create an opportunity for some meaningful upside in the second half of this year.

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, European Wax Center might not be the best prospect out there. But it is an interesting value play that I view as a turnaround candidate. The improvements in profitability have been immensely reassuring, though what we really need to see in order for massive upside to be on the table is a return to revenue growth as well. For now, I think that maintaining it as a soft "Buy" makes the most sense. But if we start to see some meaningful improvements, an upgrade could be in store.

data.title: Fastenal Company: Valuation Is Still High, And Earnings Could Miss Consensus Estimates
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to Fastenal Company (FAST) in July last year, as I was not a fan of the high valuation, since it meant no room for error. Fast forward to today; the strong January data is encouraging, but I don’t think it is enough for me to ignore the red flags from fading pricing leverage and slowing FMI deployment.

Earnings Results Update

Q4 2025 net sales came in at ~$2.03 billion, missing consensus, and while headline growth remained positive, the quality of that growth deteriorated. By segments, Fastener daily sales grew 10.8% y/y, with direct fasteners up 12.1%, while indirect fasteners were down 1.9%. As for non-fastener categories, they did better, with Safety up 9.2% y/y and Other products up 11.8% y/y. But overall, margins were rather muted. Gross margin fell to 44.3% due to inventory overhang and rebate timing, but with SG&A cost control, EBIT margin was held flattish (19% vs. Q4 2024). As a result, the miss at the top line led to a miss at the diluted EPS line, which landed at $0.26.

January Data was Positive

I believe the big green flag for this quarter’s update is the January 2026 trading data. Putting aside the lackluster Q4 numbers, I think this is the single most important data point for the bull case right now. Historically (ex-COVID period in 2020), FAST’s daily sales fell by an average of ~7% sequentially from December to January due to seasonality. That did not happen this year. January daily sales were actually up 1% versus December. This is a massive swing, and the inference here is that the weakness we saw in Q4 may be a transitory issue rather than a structural one. The logic is that if underlying demand is really weak, and given there are no significant macro developments to push a sharp recovery, that weakness should roll over, and you would not see this kind of January strength. As such, what this potentially suggests is that end-market demand is re-accelerating in 2026. If this trend persists, I think the market will very quickly forget about this Q4 weakness and start to price in stronger growth for FY2026.

Concerns on Valuation and Pricing Power

While the January data was encouraging, I believe there are other negative points that offset this positive data point. Firstly, FAST’s pricing power is showing signs of weakness. In Q4 2025, pricing contributed ~310 to 340 bps to growth, and if you don’t look beyond the numbers, it sounded really good. But what matters here is management’s comment. They explicitly used the word “fatigue” and slower-than-expected implementation of price increases, particularly in non-fastener categories.

The former tells us that FAST is likely seeing some pushback from clients when it comes to pricing; the latter tells us a sizable chunk of FAST’s business (non-Fasteners) will not be able to grow as much anymore. Even more concerning is the fact that the price/cost dynamic (the spread between what FAST pays for goods and what they charge customers) flipped negative to ~-10 bps for Q4. This tells you a lot about the fading pricing power, as FAST could not raise prices fast enough to cover their own rising costs and had to eat the difference.

If FAST can no longer push price to offset cost inflation, the burden of growth shifts entirely to volume, which is far more cyclical (really dependent on industrial and manufacturing activity that follows the macro cycle). And that should bring down the valuation multiple since a cyclical company with bigger margin swings deserves a lower multiple.

Now, on volume, the problem I have with assuming strong growth here is that Fastenal Managed Inventory [FMI] demand is simply not great. I have already flagged previously that the cut in FMI device guidance to 20,000 to 22,000 units was a red flag. Q4 2025 confirms my worry as weighted FMI device signings fell to 5,966 in the quarter, down 12.1% y/y. That is a sharp deceleration and, more importantly, confirms the trend that vending signings have effectively turned negative (are down over the last twelve months). As I said before, FMI devices are a key part of FAST’s moat and growth model since they physically embed FAST into customer workflows, drive recurring revenue, and increase the switching cost. The fact that customers are not deploying as many FMI devices tells me that they are either (1) delaying capital commitments to preserve balance sheet flexibility or (2) the underlying demand is so weak that it doesn’t justify setting it up now.

You put these two together: fading pricing power and a shrinking pipeline of new FMI device deployment; they translate to weak growth ahead.

Valuation

The valuation concern I had previously still persists. Previously, I had the view that FAST was expensive at 38.7x forward earnings, but despite delivering a horrible quarter of triple misses on sales, margins, and earnings, the multiple is still at 36x (above the +1 standard deviation level over the past 10 years). I get that January data was good, but let’s be honest, that is really one month of data that doesn’t spell a trend. The trend that we can verify and look back on is that FMI device deployment is down, and the factual data we have is that there is pricing fatigue. And yet, consensus is expecting pretty much no major slowdown in growth over the next few quarters.

I believe consensus may be too optimistic in their estimates, and this sets the risk/reward to skew towards the downside. I would state that consensus estimates have not been the most accurate in the recent quarters. Even if you think consensus growth estimates are accurate, the last time FAST had this ~10+% adj. EPS growth, the stock only traded at 27x forward P/E (the historical average). Unless there is a strong catalyst for FAST’s growth to accelerate significantly in the near term, so much that it justifies a much higher multiple. I don’t think this deserves a buy rating today.

Conclusion

I stick to my view that FAST deserves a hold rating. January sales momentum is a real positive and could signal improving end-market demand, but it is being offset by two issues: (1) pricing fatigue and (2) a shrinking pipeline of FMI device deployments. I believe these two will limit near-term upside and potentially result in FAST missing consensus estimates. At ~36x forward earnings, the stock is still priced for near-perfect execution, and I don’t like the risk/reward setup.

data.title: Harrow: Revisiting This Eyecare Growth Story Ahead Of Q4 Update
data.description:
Shares of eyecare specialist Harrow, Inc. (HROW) have risen by 219% over the past three years as the company increased its focus on branded products segment and diversified away from its initial compounding business.

On the other hand, shares are down 6% in 2026 and have lost 27% of their value over the past 12 months.

Ahead of the company's Q4 report, I wanted to revisit and remind readers why Harrow remains a key holding in my Core Biotech portfolio.

Chart

Figure 1: HROW daily chart.

When looking at charts, clarity often comes from taking a look at distinct time frames in order to determine important technical levels and get a feel for what's going on. In the daily chart above, we can see shares march up from mid $20s and peak at $55, from there pulling back to mid-$40's.

This was far from a straight line-up though, as the stock was prone to selloffs followed by recoveries in prior quarterly reports (Wall Street didn't like the headline metrics such as revenue growth, only to then read CEO commentary in shareholder letter and take note of bullish notes in the call that followed).

My initial take is that Q4 report, historically released in late March by the company, could similarly see initial selloff on a revenue miss followed by recovery as growth trends & market penetration for Vevye in the lucrative dry eye disease market take center stage (and other branded products such as Iheezo contribute).

A conservative plan for investors who want more exposure to this name could be buying a quarter-size position, then only adding more exposure if said selloff occurs (perhaps to mid $30s to $40 range where the 200-day moving average currently resides).

Select Recent Developments

On October 6th, the company hired a new CEO and Head of Quality for its ImprimisRx subsidiary. Frank Mullery prior served as President of Sintetica US (leading it from creation to commercialization & launching its first five sterile injectable products). Bridseida Cruz seems to have the requisite experience as well (25+ years leading quality and operations in 503B outsourcing, also leading FDA Warning Letter and regulatory observation remediation plans). This last part seemed to spook the market, with the stock down over 20% from September highs at $50 at one point.

It later came to light that the California Board of Pharmacy filed an amended accusation against Harrow's subsidiary ImprimisRX (alleging quality control and compliance issues at its compounding and outsourcing facilities). Importantly, the California Board of Pharmacy seeks disciplinary actions and this is administrative (not criminal). As smarter minds pointed out, these events do not affect the company's FDA-branded division (center of our investment thesis with drugs like Vevye and Iheezo).

For Q3 (see slides here), $71.6M of revenue slightly missed analyst estimates while they did beat on EPS of $0.33. CEO highlighted it as another period of meaningful progress with strong performance from Vevye and Iheezo, while advancing several strategic initiatives for future growth such as upcoming HAFA (Harrow Access for All) program and new major coverage wins for Vevye. They are targeting sustainable growth for years to come (contrasts to Wall Street's focus on a narrow revenue miss).

Vevye is expected to be included on multiple new formulary listings with a preferred product status effective January 1st (nice tailwind along with VAFA partnership with full-service digital pharmacy + third specialty pharmacy partner).

Here are a few nuggets from Q3 Letter to Stockholders that stood out to me:

  • $71.6M revenue represents a 45% increase over Q3 2024 and 12% sequential increase from Q2 2025.

Figure 2: Quarterly financial metrics (Source: Q3 slides).

  • Operating leverage is evident with $1M net income and $22.7M adjusted EBITDA.
  • They are seeing strong momentum for Vevye and Iheezo, both performing above expectations and steadily gaining market share.
  • Triesence and their Rare & Specialty Products or RSP portfolio have not met expectations this year or in Q3. CEO states it's way too early to despair on Triesence- on October 1st they significantly lowered its price as it launched into the largest market opportunity of ocular inflammation. He notes that early commercial indicators are positive, including outstanding clinical outcomes, strong coverage, low prior authorization rates, and lowest-in-class patient out-of pocket costs. CEO strongly believes they are on the right track and anticipates a turnaround for RSP portfolio in Q4 and continuing into 2026 (aided by HAFA).

Figure 3: Key growth drivers for Q3 2025 (Source: Q3 slides).

  • As for capital structure, they achieved all three previously stated objectives of lowering cost of capital (reduced interest expense by $3M per year), greater financial flexibility (extended debt maturities and reduced near-term obligations) and enhanced ability to pursue growth/increased liquidity to support business development (added $50M in new liquidity). A key goal is to operate with gross leverage under 3x and net leverage under 2x (they expect to achieve this in the near term).
  • Vevye continues to build momentum-their objective is to make it the leading cyclosporine-based DED prescription product in the US market. Despite a conservative launch and investment in salesforce, Vevye revenue increased 22% quarter-over-quarter to $22.6M and achieved 10.5% share of the national dry eye disease market by the end of the quarter (based on IQVIA and PhilRx data). This represents 2.7% quarter-over-quarter gain and expect that trend of market share growth to continue. Multiple new formularies are coming online January 2026, with Vevye recently chosen by the largest U.S. pharmacy benefit manager of commercial lives as one of two preferred products. PhilRx served as primary specialty pharmacy in Q3, Apollo Care officially came online in early Q4 with Alto Rx to follow later in Q4 (addition of the latter two will further enhance patient access and increase the proportion of patients receiving insurance coverage).

Figure 4: Bullish outlook for Vevye as it takes share in dry eye (Source: Q3 slides).

  • They saw a modest decline in Vevye's net price per unit (NPP) but it remains within modeled range, and they expect it to firm up in the near-term.
  • With the above changes, they expect a higher proportion of patients accessing and receiving insurance coverage in 2026.
  • The potent combination of NRx/TRx growth, improved coverage and a lower percentage of cash pay prescriptions makes 2026 particularly exciting for Vevye.
  • Investments in supply chain are bearing fruit as well (they are now comfortable with current safety stock levels to ensure product availability to meet rising demand). With increased manufacturing volumes scheduled to come online in 2026, they've initially targeted opening 10 new Vevye territories and plan to invest further in commercial infrastructure to fuel the next phase of expansion and sustained growth through the remainder of the decade. They expect to open more territories during 1H 26, focusing on market clusters served by the new plans that will cover Vevye.
  • Vevye is on pace for a record Q4, October was the strongest month since launch with the highest number of prescriptions written to date. They expect to finish the year strong, coming in at or near the full-year revenue target for 2025 (devil's advocate, this sounds like it could be a slight miss).
  • They also added FLAREX and FRESHKOTE to the bag of their DED team, which strengthens their clinical offering providing a complete suite of therapies to manage ocular surface disease across the full spectrum of inflammation and dryness. Vevye provides long-term management for DED, FLAREX provides an effective option for short acute phases of anterior segment inflammation and FRESHKOTE complements both by protecting the corneal surface and stabilizing the tear film to reduce evaporation.
  • Again, the theme is being all about the portfolio overall (management always adding complementary products).
  • Iheezo revenue increased +70% to $21.9M vs Q3 2024, a 20% sequential increase vs Q2 2025. Q3 unit demand of 50,565 represents 47% increase over Q4 2024 and 4% sequential increase over Q2 2025. It was seasonally slower in Q3 as patients and physicians take time off in July/August slowdown, but demand rebounded sharply in September, and momentum carried into October with notably large new orders placed. They are positioned for a strong finish to 2026 and continued growth in 2026. 46% of total accounts that have ordered Iheezo are new to the product in 2025. They are easily on track to surpass $50M in annual revenue by year-end 2025.
  • Triesence achieved 67% quarter over quarter unit growth. 53% of accounts ordering were new customers. October they launched into the ocular inflammation market. Surgical markets tend to dip their toes into the water (order a few trade units to ensure a good experience), ensure coverage and only then make a financial commitment to purchasing larger volumes. Once adopted, there can be a "positive snowball cycle" for many years as physicians grow reluctant to switch away from what works and teach younger doctors. Early utilization is showing positive indicators of market traction.
  • NDA submission for MELT-300 1H 27 positions them for potential commercial launch in 2028 (working to accelerate that timeline).
  • ImprimisRx compounded products continue to provide revenue stability, generating $20.1M in Q3. Inventory shortage was encountered in October, and they won't be able to make it up (slightly reducing overall guidance for the year as a result due to this one-time revenue loss). They are now rebuilding inventory to an appropriate safety stock level and brought in Frank Mullery as new CEO of ImprimisRx to shore things up.
  • There could be a path to achieving third consecutive year of 40%+ revenue growth, but they chose a slightly more conservative approach and lowered guidance to what they believe they can deliver ($270M to $280M full-year revenue).
  • Vevye is setting up for a very powerful 2026, Iheezo remains on track to exceed expectations, initial Byqlovi inventories are a few months away, and they are preparing to introduce Byooviz in mid-2026 and Opuviz in mid-2027. Eyes wide open, Triesence and RSP portfolio have underperformed this year, but they anticipate a meaningful rebound in Q4 continuing into 2026.
  • As one would expect, Q1 2026 will see a seasonal decline.

Net-net, to my eyes the story looks well on track even if the market is overly concerned about a slight revenue miss and lowering of guidance for 2026 (I prefer this candid approach from management as they don't sugarcoat their shortcomings and where improvement needs to occur).

Here are a few additional nuggets from the Q3 conference call (I think the shareholder letter covered most of it):

  • There is clear evidence of operating leverage in their business model.
  • We can see a nice tailwind for Vevye with preferred product status in the largest U.S. pharmacy benefit manager starting in January 2026.
  • Vevye captured 10.5% of the total dry eye market, doubling its market share in just two quarters!
  • More territories for Vevye will open in 2026.
  • We can see a great adjusted EBITDA just under $23M, as the company generated about $16M of cash from operations in Q3.
  • Additional OpEx is revenue-generating OpEx, so we should see an immediate return on it.
  • They are making moderate investments in commercial infrastructure (not huge), investment should see almost immediate impact and return on revenue.
  • Management expects the bias toward improving ASP (average selling price) for Vevye and potentially improving "more than modestly" as ratio of covered vs cash pay begins to change.
  • Commercial lives are the most attractive lives they can get (i.e. if new coverage gets $20 improvement in ASP, it affects every single one of those units).
  • They are very conservative in how they invest commercially. You won't see extravagant launches, as they tend to dip their toes in and see what works over time.
  • They expect to see improvements in Vevye ASP in 2026.
  • Preferred status with the largest PBM lets them reach out to patients who have been denied coverage from those plans, are paying cash and transition them to covered (in some cases, no-copay type coverage).
  • More volume, more demand is a cycle that in turn gives them more leverage to negotiate with payers in 2026, 2027 and so on.
  • The exciting thing about Q4 is the emergence of Triesence finally (time for orders, revenues, reorders, new accounts adopting).
  • The CEO sees Triesence as a nine-figure product, which sounds delusional now, but he thinks this time next year it won't look like that.
  • CEO states people don't really understand the value of Melt Pharmaceuticals, but they have a transformative vision for ophthalmic surgery which is to have opioid free and drop-free cataract surgery. Main idea is being able to go into an office, have no IVs, no opioids, not need eye drops post-surgery, which to my eyes is extraordinary. This would be transformative for the nearly 5 million surgeries that occur annually in the U.S. Global opportunity, and they will discuss with partners for other territories.

On November 18th, the company announced completion of its acquisition of Melt Pharmaceuticals and again highlighted the outsized market opportunity for MELT-300 (achieved statistical superiority to midazolam alone in phase 2 and 3 clinical programs). The asset fits into the key niche and growing demand from healthcare system to reduce exposure to opioids and could be incorporated into a wide range of office-based and outpatient procedures. Harrow will also advance the MELT-210 drug candidate in procedural sedation (sublingual dosage of midazolam using Zydis ODT technology, has advantageous characteristics such as rapid uptake and short half-life). KOL also highlights enthusiasm for needle-free sedation, no need for opioids or IV access, etc.

As for next steps, the company will initiate non-clinical animal study and three PK studies to prepare for NDA submission 1H 27 with regulatory and approval decision potentially 1H 28 (commercial launch 2H 28). Thus, this is an asset for long-term growth but little in the way of impact in the near term (where our focus remains on current branded product portfolio spearheaded by Vevye).

Final Thoughts

To conclude, with enterprise value of ~$1.9 billion, HROW remains a Buy and I continue to hold my position patiently for accelerating sales growth expected over the 2026 to 2028 period (and beyond).

While there could be some volatility on a potential revenue miss, Vevye remains on pace for a record Q4 and synergies of their branded products segment should become increasingly evident later this year. Additionally, CEO has shown they are cost conscious with business development as they license in complementary eyecare assets on the cheap and plug them into existing infrastructure.

On the con side, Harrow is not one of the "shiny new objects" currently being chased in biotech (unsexy non-binary profile, not depending on a single readout where it could be zero or hero depending on the outcome).

For my purposes, I prioritize owning such names (high upside potential, significant downside cushion) as they allow me to sleep well at night (if such a thing were to exist in biotech).

For the conservative investor, a potential action plan could be buying partial size presently and only adding more exposure if there's a "sell the news" reaction to Q4 report (viewing the resulting pullback as an opportunity if you have a multi-year timeframe).

Key risks include any legal action that takes place for their compounding unit, overpaying for future business development or any new products they license in, a cash position that could still use some strengthening (perhaps more dilution is in the cards at some point in 2026) and slowing sales or disappointing penetration for Vevye or other branded products where Harrow is competing against larger and far better established pharmaceutical companies.

data.title: Campbell's: Margins Under Pressure, But There's Upside At Today's Depressed Multiple
data.description:
Introduction

Shares of The Campbell's Company (CPB) have been under stress over the last twelve months, having fallen 30% from highs made early last year. Over this time period, the market has reassessed its growth, leverage post-deals, and sentiment in "old" packaged food names have turned sour. Fundamentally, the company's organic growth has slowed considerably and margins have also compressed as the company faces high competition and heavy promo intensity.

But in my view, beneath the near-term pressure is what I think is an increasingly attractive risk/reward setup. Fiscal Q1 '26 disappointed investors, but the valuation has become compelling. The company now trades at 8.4x EV/EBITDA and investors are getting a 6% yield that's is well covered. With leverage manageable at 3.7x, cash flow stable, and depressed multiples already pricing in continued softness, I think the downside is limited. Modest volume stabilization, margin progress, or even a return to mid-teens valuation could drive meaningful total returns which is why I rate CPB shares a Buy.

A poor Q1 on margins

Campbell’s Q1 was a disappointment for investors who have been waiting for a turnaround. Over the last several quarters, the company has been putting up weak sales and EPS figures that have missed sell-side expectations.

For the first quarter, Campbell’s revenue came in at $2.68 billion, down 1% compared to last year largely driven by 2% a drop in consumption through retailers’-built inventory in snacks ahead of promotions. Including the divestitures like noosa and Pop Secret, net sales was down 3%, as shown below. About 3% of the drop came from Volume/Mix while the company had +1% net price realization across the company.

When we look at Campbell's profitability for the quarter, the company’s EBIT was -11% as gross margins compressed. In my view the issues are really two-fold.

First, Snacks’ organic sales in the quarter was better than consumption, and consumption is deteriorating in a year when the company expects Snacks to get better. On the earnings call, management noted it shipped 1pt ahead of consumption and they expect to give back this shipment timing benefit in Q2. Management also noted that the company needs to see more proof points come together in Q2 to inform the back half of the year, currently seen as “flattish” (CEO Beekhuizzen).

The second issue is on margins. On gross margins, Q1 gross margins were at their lowest level ever and it seems like Q2 will be yet lower which is all the more interesting in a quarter when price was climbing. The net here is sales and margin tension.

Tariffs alone took about 200bps out of gross margin (dropping it to 29.9%), while broader cost inflation added another big hit. Even with efforts on the supply-chain side that recouped ~70% of the pressure, the net result was a 150bps decline year over year. The competitive environment in snacks made it harder to push through price increases without losing volume and earlier pricing in ready-to-serve soups triggered some elasticity that hurt top-line growth.

Where does the company go from here as investors look to Q2?

Looking ahead, I’d expect similar pressure in Q2 and investors should brace for continued margin pressure and a still-challenging operating environment. Management has indicated that adjusted gross margins will likely decline by another 150bps, slightly more year over year because of persistent input cost inflation, the full weight of tariffs, and higher promotional activity aimed at supporting volumes and gaining traction in a selective consumer landscape. Snacks will probably remain the tougher segment, with competitive dynamics because of affordability keeping share recovery efforts against competitors gradual. While I have the view that more progress toward the $375 million cost-savings target by 2028 should start lifting margins, though meaningful expansion will likely take time and depend on stabilizing volumes.

Gross margin has been tough to call. Overall, accelerated pricing may protect margins despite higher inflation/softer volumes. While this hasn’t yet played out as pricing was offset by inflation it seems gross margins get worse (seasonality and inflation) before they get better. The good news is that this is likely what the market is already expecting and being priced into the stock.

Most investors like to invest in great businesses with accelerating top lines with margin expansion. That’s not what’s going on here but that doesn’t mean that Campbell’s is necessarily a bad investment. With operating earnings in the company’s Meals & Beverages segment down 13% with margins down 190bps (again, mostly from tariffs and gross profit pressure of 280bps), there’s real headwinds coming from lower sales comps and margin contraction.

The bright spot here is on valuation

Ultimately, I think you’re getting Campbell’s at an attractive enough valuation to compensate you for the risks. Downside risk is minimal here thanks to the low multiple especially when you compare CPB to peers and its own historical valuation. You don’t need a margin uplift in 2026 for shares to work from here in my view.

One of the slides from Campbell’s investor deck that I think is worth pointing out is on the company’s cash flow generation. While capex is higher than last year and share repurchases were lower, the company’s net cash flows from operations is roughly unchanged year over year. With the leverage ratio at 3.7x, leverage is high but EBITDA hasn’t fallen off that much. FY’25 EBITDA was $3.14 billion and consensus estimates for FY’26 is $2.91 billion (source: Bloomberg).

Even though EBITDA has trended lower over the last year, I do think investors can earn solid returns through both dividends and capital appreciation. On the dividend side, the dividend looks healthy with a modest payout ratio of 54.6%. With a yield of 6.0% that’s well covered by the company’s earnings generation ($2.84 of EPS generated in the LTM period), investors are getting paid to wait for growth beyond 2027 and a rerating of the valuation multiple.

When looking at the valuation, historically, Campbell’s has traded within a range of 8.3x and 15.4x EV/EBITDA. Today, that multiple stands at 8.4x., close to the low end of the range.

To further illustrate the point that Campbell’s is undervalued, it’s worth looking at where the company stacks up relative to peers. As shown below, the company trades at a near 4-turn discount to the average multiple in the space of around 14.0x. To me, that’s far too wide of a discount to competitors when pressures are mostly industry felt and the fact that the company’s LTM numbers are closer to depressed earnings, rather than what would otherwise be considered "run-rate."

If we have any kind of growth in FY’27 and beyond, Campbell’s is certainly going to look quite cheap. At 10.7x forward P/E, even an average growth rate of 8% in EPS will likely bring this to a 7x multiple stock and so it’s quite possible the stock could be a double 4-5 years from now if the company is able to get back to a 14x P/E which it traded at the beginning of last year.

Conclusion

Current investors are no doubt disappointed on the near-term performance of the stock as the company is still challenged on margins. Campbell’s isn't out of the woods yet. That said, I believe much of this pessimism is already reflected in the stock. The company is navigating a difficult cost and promotional environment, but it continues to generate resilient cash flow, maintain a well-covered dividend, and operate with leverage that is manageable rather than precarious. At trough multiples, investors are being compensated for waiting in this turnaround story and any progressive on volumes, execution on cost savings or normalization in margins would be catalysts for a rerating. In that context, Campbell’s may not be a growth story today, but at the current valuation it looks like an attractive, contrarian opportunity with asymmetric upside over a multi-year time horizon.

data.title: Blackstone's BREIT: Private Equity Outperformance Is Not What It Appears
data.description:
Blackstone’s (BX) real estate investment vehicle BREIT claims to have an 8.1% return in 2025, which would represent sizable outperformance over public REITs. The average REIT was down in 2025, and even cap-weighted indexes, such as the Vanguard REIT ETF (VNQ), that benefited from mega-cap REIT outperformance, had quite modest returns. VNQ returned 4.17% inclusive of dividends.

Blackstone suggests that its outperformance stems from superior asset selection. They directly compare their performance to that of publicly traded REITs and claim 70% outperformance.

Note this is 70% higher, not 70 percentage points.

While I admire Blackstone’s salesmanship and ability to raise tremendous amounts of AUM, I want to set the record straight on their performance.

Total return is a very different figure for public REITs than it is for private REITs.

Private Versus Public—What Total Return Means

For public markets, there is an established methodology for calculating returns, and it can be verified by the market prices of the stocks. For an individual stock, one can calculate total return by comparing the price at the end of 2025 to the price at the start of 2025 and adding dividends. A portfolio’s performance can be measured similarly by taking the sum product of each position’s return and the weights.

For active portfolios, there is a bit more to it, as one has to factor in trade dates and pricing at the time of each trade, but this has all been standardized. Returns are a factual figure that can be verified.

Private equity has a bit more leeway in the way they report returns, as it is not based on a visible barometer but rather their internal evaluation of NAV. As such, the 2025 total return of BREIT is a comparison of what Blackstone thought those assets were worth at the end of 2024 and what Blackstone thinks they are worth at the end of 2025.

Both figures are a bit fuzzier and harder to verify than they are with public markets.

We think Blackstone’s use of the term outperformance is misleading because it is not an apples-to-apples comparison. They are comparing estimated asset value (BREIT NAV) to public market pricing (publicly traded REITs).

If we dig a bit deeper, I think it becomes clear that this “outperformance” is a meaningless figure. It says quite a bit more about the spread between public REIT prices and NAV of real estate than it does about BREIT’s performance.

Asset Weights and How Each Asset Type Performed

BREIT puts out a nice breakdown of their property sector weights.

We shall now look at the performance of these various asset classes.

As seen on the graph above, Rental housing, industrial, and data centers are the top exposures for BREIT.

Single-family rental was a tough sector in 2025. A wave of supply put pressure on asking rents. We go into greater detail here. As stated in that article, we may look to get into single-family rental now that the pricing on the sector is much cheaper, but it was not a good place to be at the start of 2025. American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) and Invitation Homes (INVH) were each down substantially in 2025.

Multifamily apartments are another big contingent of BREIT’s rental housing. That sector was also tough due to a similar supply wave. Asking rents for apartments nationally were flat to slightly down in 2025. As one would expect in such an environment, the big apartment players were down significantly, with Camden (CPT), Equity Residential (EQR), and Avalon Bay (AVB) down 4%, 10%, and 16%, respectively.

Industrial appears to be the bright spot in BREIT’s portfolio. While industrial had a similar supply wave, there was enough positive mark-to-market of rent that NOI for the sector still increased nicely. Industrial REITs were up about 17% in 2025.

Data centers are a major component of BREIT at 21% of their portfolio. While the sector is hot due to the AI buildout, valuation matters, and at the start of 2025, data centers were trading at sky-high valuations. One could buy data centers as Blackstone did, but to do so, you had to pay a massive premium. Flocking to the hot sector comes with quite a price tag.

Thus, even though data center fundamentals are strong, the sector suffered in 2025. The biggest and best players traded down severely. Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty (DLR) dropped 19% and 13%, respectively, in 2025.

We like strong fundamentals, but we also like value, so we waited until AFTER the price drop in Equinix to jump in.

The other large portions of BREIT are student housing and affordable housing. There is no clear public proxy for student housing as the last student housing REIT, American Campus Communities (formerly ACC), went private in 2022.

The best proxy for affordable housing would be manufactured housing and RVs, with the large-cap REITs in the space being Sun Communities (SUI) and Equity Lifestyle (ELS). Each performed rather poorly in 2025.

BREIT’s “Outperformance” is Really Just a Differing Reporting Methodology

In looking at each of BREIT's main portfolio concentrations, only industrial performed well in 2025. Data centers, apartments, single-family rentals, and affordable housing were all down substantially in 2025.

So how does Blackstone come up with the +8.1% figure?

It comes down to a methodology difference. Real estate fundamentally performed well in 2025. The weakness in publicly traded REITs was related to them getting cheaper relative to the underlying value of their assets.

Publicly traded REITs now trade at a massive discount to net asset value. The median REIT trades at 83% of NAV. Many trade for less than 70% of NAV.

Thus, it is not that BREIT outperformed on a fundamental or NAV basis. Instead, publicly traded REITs simply traded down relative to their NAV.

It strikes me as reasonable that the asset value of BREIT could have risen by 8.1% (inclusive of dividends paid) in 2025. It is not inherently an outrageous figure.

Real estate in general tends to return north of 8% annually. Publicly traded equity REITs averaged compound annual returns of 9.3% over the last 40 years.

In 2025, publicly traded REITs gained somewhere in the ballpark of 8%-9% in fundamental asset value.

The difference is that public REIT returns are reported based on market pricing while BREIT’s returns are reported based on estimated asset value.

Thus, I do not see it as outperformance. Both BREIT and public REITs performed reasonably well fundamentally. The delta in the 2025 return is simply a difference in reporting methodology.

In this article, we compared BREIT’s sectors to the publicly traded performance of the highest caliber REITs.

BREIT employs intelligent people for asset selection. I have no doubt they do a competent job of choosing which properties to buy.

However, in my opinion:

  • It is unrealistic to think their data center selection and management are superior to that of Equinix, which is the preeminent global leader in data centers.
  • It is unrealistic to think their single-family rental platform is better than those of Invitation Homes and American Homes 4 Rent, which boast the highest margins in the business.
  • It is unrealistic to think BREIT can better acquire and manage industrial real estate than behemoths like Prologis (PLD), who literally wrote the white papers that define the sector.

Outperformance of private equity is merely an illusion created by volatile market pricing of publicly traded stocks.

The sales team at Blackstone is correct to spin it as outperformance. That is their job.

It is the job of investors to see through it and invest in what is most opportunistic at any given moment.

BREIT is an entirely reasonable vehicle. Their fees are a bit high, but it is generally an okay place to invest.

However, timing matters, and the worst time to invest in private equity is when it is priced at a huge premium to public equivalents.

Why buy shares of BREIT at 100% of NAV (before fees and above 100% after fees) when you can buy high-quality publicly traded companies at large discounts to NAV? Private equity REITs generally underperform public equity REITs, and the underperformance is greater when the private vehicles are trading at a premium to the public vehicles.

This pattern has played out before.

In December 2022, we had similar gripes with the way BREIT was priced at a vast premium to public REITs.

“Over time, public and private valuations realign, and that means there will be a 30% delta in the other direction. Either private market NAVs are coming down or public market REITs are coming up or some combination of the 2.”

Indeed, BREIT has performed quite poorly since December 2022.

BREIT Performance

Source: Blackstone

We view today as a similar setup.

BREIT is trading at a premium to public companies in the same asset classes and is thus positioned to underperform going forward.

The Actionable Conclusion

Shares of BREIT are redeemable, and importantly, redemption is at the NAV stated by Blackstone.

This allows investors to cash out at what is functionally full NAV and reinvest that capital in public REITs in the same property sectors at much more attractive valuations.

Impact on Blackstone

While I think investors would be wise to consider cashing out of private equity, there is no reason to believe that is actually going to happen at a mass scale.

Blackstone has proven time and time again that they are a capital-raising machine. They are remarkably adept at raising AUM, and that is ultimately the driver of BX. Thus, I am bearish on BREIT and "Neutral" on BX.

data.title: Dorian LPG: Accumulating A Small Holding
data.description:
Shares of very large gas carrier (VLGC) owner-operator Dorian LPG Ltd. (LPG) have rallied 20% since Christmas Eve, spurred by an analyst upgrade citing $70,000 day rates in January 2026. The market is extremely volatile and should remain so as the U.S. is poised to increase its export capacity by 66% from 2024 to 2028 while 108 vessels are scheduled to be delivered 2026 to 2028. With a good balance sheet, an irregular dividend yield of 8.6% over the past 12 months, and potentially bullish Panama Canal dynamics owing to several factors, Dorian merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Dorian LPG, Ltd. is a Marshall Islands domiciled, Stamford, Connecticut headquartered liquified petroleum gas (mainly propane) shipping concern focused on the operation of its 27 very large gas carriers (VLGCs) with an aggregate carrying capacity of ~2.3 million cubic meters (cbm). The company owns 21 of its vessels and is expected to take delivery of a new VLGC in spring 2026. Dorian was founded in 2013 and went public in 2014, raising net proceeds of $127.9 million at $19 per share. Its stock trades around $28.00 a share, translating to an approximate market cap of $1.2 billion.

The company operates on a fiscal year (FY) ending March 31st. For the avoidance of doubt, the 12-month period ending March 31, 2026 is FY26. Unless otherwise noted, day rate pricing references below are in calendar quarters or years.

VLGC Market

The VLGC market in which Dorian participates is relatively small but not terribly concentrated. As of May 23, 2025, its 27 vessels accounted for 7% of a total market consisting of 412 freighters with an aggregate carrying capacity of 33.6 million cbm. The global VLGC fleet is owned by a total of 115 entities worldwide, with the top ten players responsible for 41% of the count. The company lists BW LPG Ltd (BWLP), Nippon Yusen Kabushiki Kaisha (NPNYY), and Petredec Pte. Ltd. as its largest competitors.

For the sake of clarity, Dorian does not transport liquefied natural gas, which is primarily methane. The main liquified petroleum gas route [LPG] had traditionally originated in the Arabian Gulf (Ras Tanura, Saudi Arabia) destined for markets in Asia. However, with evolving dynamics related to the Ukrainian conflict and the emergence of the U.S. as the largest export hub – production up 90% since 2015 – Texas to Europe and Asia routes have grown in importance. Unsurprisingly, Dorian’s performance is a function of supply-demand dynamics in the LPG transport industry, which is an affected by a plethora of factors discussed below.

Vessels are generally operated under time charters, bareboat charters, spot charters, or contracts of affreightment (COAs). Time charters involve the charterer (customer) contracting a vessel for a fixed period at set daily or monthly rates, paying voyage expenses such as fuel and port costs. Bareboat charters entail the charterer taking full control of the vessel for a predetermined period, baring full responsibility for the crew and fuel. Spot charters involve the charterer contracting for the transport of LPG from load port to discharge port at an agreed upon freight per ton of cargo carried. In spot market charters, Dorian bears the port and fuel costs. COAs are somewhat of a hybrid, entailing the carriage of multiple cargoes over the same or multiple routes at pre-arranged terms, where Dorain essentially handles all the logistics, whether with its own vessels or others, with voyage and capital costs borne by the shipowner. At the risk of stating the obvious, a portfolio weighted heavily in time charters provides more predictable cash flows vis a vie spot charters, but typically generates lower revenue in rising day rate environments.

Joint Venture & Fleet

In an attempt to better cover its customer base from a geographic perspective back in 2015, Dorian entered into a joint venture with Singapore based MOL Energia (known as the Helios Pool) into which the former has committed its entire fleet and the latter three vessels. The pool can operate in the spot market, pursuant to COAs, or time charters of two years or less. That said, Dorian operates almost exclusively in the spot market with customers ranging from oil giants like Exxon Mobil (XOM) or Chevron (CVX) to commodity traders like Glencore plc (GLCNF) or importers such as Indian Oil.

The average age of the company’s fleet is nine years versus an industry average of ten. Sixteen of the company’s fleet are equipped with sulfur oxide exhaust gas cleaning systems known as scrubbers, which allow them to consume cheaper, high-sulfur-content marine fuel, improving voyage economics. As stated above, it anticipates onboarding a new VLGC in calendar 2Q26, one of 36 expected to be delivered across the industry in calendar 2026.

Share Price Performance

Owing to the volatility of energy complex pricing and finite number of vessels in service, day rates in the VLGC market can oscillate wildly, typically trading lower in the first half of a calendar year and rising in the second half as importers in the Northern Hemisphere build inventories for the winter heating season. Shipping prices are influenced by several factors, including U.S. propane production and consumption, export capacity, the spread between the U.S. (Mont Belvieu) propane market and prices paid in the Far East end markets, and maybe most importantly, sailing patterns – notably dictated by operational capacity at the Panama Canal, through which ~75% of U.S. to Far East LPG shipments transit. With the Permian Basin producing more gas as it ages, the supply for export will not be an issue, especially with U.S. inventories of propane and propylene hitting 23-year highs in late-October 2025. Another variable to factor into the future algebra is the expected addition of 108 vessels with 9.7 million cbm of capacity expected to be delivered from 2026 to YE28, which will be needed to handle the expected 66% jump in U.S. export capacity (2024 to 2028) eventually reaching 113 million tonnes per annum.

Returning to the volatility, on a time-charter equivalent (TCE) basis, day rates traded below $20,000 in June 2020, peaking at an all-time high above $170,000 in September 2023, only to spend most of 2024 between $25,000 and $50,000. 2025 was characterized by uncertainty created by U.S. tariff policy. That said, average day rates rose gradually from ~$31,000 in 1Q25 to ~$55,700 in 4Q25. Reflecting these price swings, Dorian’s stock rallied 750% off of its pandemic-selloff low to $51.66 per share in May 2024. That peak signified a top in market expectations regarding continuing momentum in day rates after the company’s record FY24 (ending March 31, 2024), when it realized an average day rate of $62,129. However, when the VLGC market faded, Dorian’s performance suffered, experiencing a 36% drop in its average day rate during FY25 to $39,778 while its daily operating expenses rose 6% to $11,143, resulting in a 51% downdraft in Adj. EBITDA to $206.0 million. Its stock reflected these realities, bottoming at $16.66 per share shortly after President Trump’s Liberation Day comments spurred uncertainty in international commerce markets during early April 2025.

2QFY26 Financials

Dorian’s stock has since rebounded along with day rates, although its most recent quarterly report of November 6, 2025 was not well-received. The company posted 2QFY26 earnings of $1.31 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $85.7 million on revenue of $123.0 million versus $0.35 per share (non-GAAP) on Adj. EBITDA of $46.2 million on revenue of $81.7 million in 2QFY25, representing somewhat gaudy year-over-year gains of 274%, 85%, and 51% respectively. These improvements were a function of its average day rate improving 45% over the prior year period to $53,725 while daily vessel operating expenses increased 6% to $10,705. However, the market was anticipating day rates in the high 50s and sold shares of LPG 8% lower to $27.17 in the subsequent trading session, eventually bottoming at a six-month low of $23.76 on December 24, 2025.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Despite the market’s disappointment, Dorian increased its ‘irregular’ dividend from $0.60 per share in August 2025 to $0.65 per share in November 2025. Although management will not call it a regular dividend owing to the capriciousness of the VLGC market, it has made four quarterly disbursements every year since achieving that feat in calendar 2022, featuring $1.00 quarterly dividends (save one $2.50 per share exception in May 2022) until falling to $0.70 per share in February 2025. Using its total irregular dividends of $2.45 per share paid in calendar 2025, Dorian’s stock currently yields 8.6%.

As far as its ability to continue to pay its irregular dividend, its balance sheet is in solid stead, reflecting cash and equivalents of $268.3 million versus debt of $526.4 million for net leverage of 1.25, with an additional $150 million of liquidity available through untapped debt financing. The last twelve months of dividends were easily covered by Dorian’s TTM Adj. EBITDA of $206.2 million versus calendar 2025 dividend payments of ~$105.0 million. The company’s board authorized a $100 million share repurchase program in 2022, but it has only bought back $5.6 million of stock since its inception.

After an upgrade from Pareto Securities on January 6, 2026, the Street leans positive on Dorian’s prospects. On average, sell-side analysts expect the company to earn $4.09 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $412.4 million in FY26, followed by $2.69 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $363.2 million in FY27 as more vessels come online in calendar 2026.

CEO, President & Chairman John Hadjipateras is bullish on his company’s outlook, purchasing 15,000 shares at $27.30 on January 7, 2026. The last time he added to his position was in April 2025, wisely using Liberation Day concerns as an opportunity to buy 12,500 shares at $17.75.

Verdict:

Whether his timing is as good as it was before remains to be seen. His stock has already snapped back 20% since Christmas Eve, somewhat bolstered by the Pareto Securities upgrade with analyst Eirik Haavaldsen citing current day rates around $70,000 and what he perceives as modest fleet growth until the back end of calendar 2026 as reasons for his optimism. To be sure, with solid demand out of China, India, and Indonesia and Enterprise Products (EPD) expected to bring its two terminal projects on line in 2026 – one in 1H26 and the other by YE26 – expanding U.S. export capacity by 25%, operating almost exclusively in the spot market should prove fruitful, although the market is subject to bouts of lumpiness with 36 new vessels hitting the market throughout 2026 and Enterprise’s second project still 11 months away.

That said, there are two more dynamics that should work in the VLGC market’s (and by extension, Dorian’s) favor. First, approximately 10% of the global fleet is over 25 years old, which means that although 108 vessels should enter the market over the next three years, ~40 will likely be coming off the market. Second, Panama Canal bottlenecks, which occurred in 2025 are bullish for VLGCs because longer time to move cargo means fewer ships competing for next shipment. There are two factors that should keep the Panama Canal backed up. Notably, it fully implemented a long-term slot allocation system in 2025, auctioning off 40% of its slots in advance, somewhat crowding out non-booked ships. This system will continue (with modifications) in 2026. Also, VLECs, which transport ethane from U.S. to China, are becoming an increasingly large consumer of the canal, averaging eight ships per month in 2025, further congesting the passage. And with continued growth of export capacity out of the U.S. and more vessels coming on the market, more of them will be compelled to take the much longer Cape of Good Hope route to the Far East, potentially putting a floor in the VLGC market.

As such, I have established a small position in LPG with covered call orders. I will look for any pull back under $25 to be used an opportunity to add slightly to the holding, as LPG feels like an Accumulate here.

data.title: Adient: There Are Cheaper Alternatives In 2026
data.description:
I generally like companies that specialize in a segment of production, such as seating. When it comes to things like aerospace interiors, I actually look for companies that do their seating and how to invest in them, both for private jets as well as for other companies. Adient plc (ADNT) is a company I reviewed almost exactly 6 months ago and which has underperformed the market since that article, per my expectations at the time.

In this article, I will review the reasons for this performance (in particular that drop we saw towards the end of the year, followed by a slight surge), as well as what sort of upside can be expected from the company from here on out. It's important to note, however, that Adient's lack of appeal to me is due to a relatively peer-heavy space - yes, even in the seating subsegment.
